title: The Goose
content:
I knew an old wife lean and poor,
Her rags scarce held together;
There strode a stranger to the door,
And it was windy weather.

He held a goose upon his arm,
He utterï¿½d rhyme and reason:
ï¿½Here, take the goose, and keep you warm
It is a stormy season.ï¿½

She caught the white goose by the leg,
A gooseï¿½ï¿½twas no great matter.
The goose let fall a golden egg
With cackle and with clatter.

She dropt the goose, and caught the pelf,
And ran to tell her neighbors,
And blessï¿½d herself, and cursed herself,
And rested from her labors;

And feeding high, and living soft,
Grew plump and able-bodied,
Until the grave churchwarden doffï¿½d,
The parson smirkï¿½d and nodded.

So sitting, served by man and maid,
She felt her heart grow prouder;
But ah! the more the white goose laid
It clackï¿½d and cackled louder.

It clutterï¿½d here, it chuckled there,
It stirrï¿½d the old wifeï¿½s mettle;
She shifted in her elbow-chair,
And hurlï¿½d the pan and kettle.

ï¿½A quinsy choke thy cursed note!ï¿½
Then waxï¿½d her anger stronger.
ï¿½Go, take the goose, and wring her throat,
I will not hear it longer.ï¿½

Then yelpï¿½d the cur, and yawlï¿½d the cat,
Ran Gaffer, stumbled Gammer.
The goose flew this way and flew that,
And fillï¿½d the house with clamor.

As head and heels upon the floor
They flounderï¿½d all together,
There strode a stranger to the door,
And it was windy weather.

He took the goose upon his arm,
He utterï¿½d words of scorning:
ï¿½So keep you cold, or keep you warm,
It is a stormy morning.ï¿½

The wild wind rang from park and plain,
And round the attics rumbled,
Till all the tables danced again,
And half the chimneys tumbled.

The glass blew in, the fire blew out,
The blast was hard and harder.
Her cap blew off, her gown blew up,
And a whirlwind clearï¿½d the larder;

And while on all sides breaking loose
Her household fled the danger,
Quoth she, ï¿½The devil take the goose,
And God forget the stranger!ï¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
